title: 10 Study Hacks That Actually Work (Not Just TikTok Trends)
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Study hacks are everywhere. Most are garbage. These 10 are backed by cognitive science and actually work. No productivity porn. No aesthetic note-taking videos. Just results.

1. The Pomodoro Technique (But With a Twist)

Standard Pomodoro: 25 minutes work, 5 minutes break. It's good, but not for everyone. Adjust the intervals to your attention span. Can't focus for 25 minutes? Start with 15/5. Can hyperfocus? Try 50/10. The key is the break — stand up, walk away, look at something far away. Don't scroll your phone. That's not a break; that's more stimulation.

2. The Feynman Technique (Explained Earlier — Do It)

Write the concept at the top of a page. Explain it in plain English. When you get stuck, that's what you don't understand. Go back to the source. Repeat. If you can't explain it to a five-year-old, you don't know it.

3. Study Before Sleep

Your brain consolidates memories during sleep. Reviewing material right before bed increases retention. Don't study intensely (that will keep you awake). Just review flashcards or skim notes for 15 minutes before lights out.

4. Change Locations

Your brain associates environment with content. Studying in the same place every day creates context-dependent memory. Change locations every few study sessions — library, coffee shop, different room at home. You'll recall information better when tested in a new environment.

5. The 5-Minute Pre-Study Ritual

Before you start, spend 5 minutes reviewing what you learned last session. This activates prior knowledge and primes your brain for new information. Students who do this remember 40% more than those who dive straight in.

6. Use Your Phone Against Itself

Your phone is your biggest distraction. Turn on grayscale mode (Settings > Accessibility > Display > Color Filters). The lack of color makes it less appealing. Also: app blockers (Freedom, Opal), Do Not Disturb, and the ultimate hack — put it in another room.

7. The Hard Start Technique

Begin with the hardest subject, even for just 5 minutes. Your willpower is highest at the start of a study session. If you start with easy tasks, you'll exhaust your willpower before reaching the hard ones. Do the hard thing first, even if you don't finish. The rest will feel easy.

8. Teach Someone Else

Volunteer to help a classmate. Start a study group where you take turns teaching. Record yourself explaining concepts. Teaching forces you to organize knowledge, find gaps, and simplify ideas. It's the most powerful form of active recall.

9. Use Background Noise Strategically

Silence is best for complex, difficult material. Brown noise (lower frequency than white noise) can help with focus for some people. Lo-fi beats work for repetitive tasks. Lyrics are almost always distracting (unless you speak another language). Experiment and find what works for you.

10. Don't Study What You Already Know

This is the most common study mistake. Students spend 80% of their time on material they've already mastered because it feels good to be right. Spend your time on what you get wrong. Study your mistakes. The discomfort of not knowing is the feeling of learning.

Bonus: The 24-Hour Rule

After a study session, don't look at the material again for 24 hours. Then test yourself. What did you forget? That's what needs reinforcement. This reveals what actually stuck versus what was just in short-term memory.

You don't need more study time. You need better study methods. Use these hacks. Your grades will thank you.
